panelReasoning: All three judges converge: 10010 in binary is 18 unsigned, and the only signed readings of a 5-bit field are -14 (two's complement), -13 (one's complement) or -2 (sign-magnitude), while a 5-bit signed field cannot even represent -27 or -37 (range -16..15), so neither surviving option is reachable under any encoding. The stem hedges itself ('i think it was') and only 2 of 4 options were recalled, so the recalled bit string is wrong and no answer can be recovered.

This is a free-recall note, not a question: no options, no key, and 'read' is a typo for 'ready'. The underlying fact is settled (a ready thread holds everything except a CPU and waits in the ready queue for the scheduler to dispatch it), but the actual question asked was never recorded, so there is nothing gradable - the item needs rewriting, not scoring.

The expansion is fixed by the ANSI standard - FDDI = Fiber Distributed Data Interface - so A is the content answer, and B/C are the classic word-swap traps. The panel splits on gradability rather than on the fact: option D is a byte-identical duplicate of A, so as printed two options are simultaneously correct and the pick of A over D is positional, not technical; the option set must be repaired before the item is used.

Unanimous: partitioning memory into equal-size blocks is paging (pages in logical memory, frames in physical), while ROM, EPROM and EEPROM are non-volatile memory technologies distinguished by erase method, so 'All of above' fails too. The stem and the option set belong to two different questions, so no option can correctly complete the stem and any key would be fabricated.

Flash is block-erasable EEPROM, which rules out ROM (mask-programmed, not rewritable) and PROM (one-time programmable) - and the only technically correct option, E, is corrupted upstream to 'E REM', leaving the true answer unreadable while the printed key points at a false option. With the sole correct choice illegible, the item is not gradable until E is restored to 'EEPROM'.

Copying program files from distribution media onto the hard disk is installation: Download and Upload are network transfers, Configuration is the post-install step, and Storage names no process - so the intended key is the fifth option, printed as the nonsense word 'Escalation'. The panel splits on whether that is a repairable typo for 'Installation' (key E) or a missing option that makes the item unanswerable as printed; the option text must be corrected before the item can be scored.

No such device class as a 'super computer floppy disc' exists, and no floppy-family format has any of the offered capacities: 3.5in HD = 1.44 MB, ED = 2.88 MB, SuperDisk LS-120 = 120/240 MB, Zip = 100/250/750 MB, while 400/500/600/700 M is the CD-ROM ladder. Only 'None of these' is vacuously defensible and the source key (500 M) is indefensible, so the premise is broken and the item should be deleted, not answered.

'Primary memory' (B) and 'main memory' (C) are exact synonyms, so RAM is equally an example of both and the source's pick of B is arbitrary between two identically true options, while D as printed ('Both (a) and (b)') is false because RAM is not secondary storage. The original almost certainly read D = 'Both (b) and (c)' with D as the key, so the recalled option set is corrupt and the item cannot be scored as printed.
